name: org.machanism.machai:genai-client
description: GenAI Client is a provider-agnostic Java library for integrating Machai applications with generative AI services. It offers a stable abstraction for prompt execution, system instructions, request-scoped conversational workflows, embedding generation, provider resolution, token-usage tracking, and controlled host-side tool exposure. The library lets applications resolve a concrete provider from identifiers such as OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini, Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet, or CodeMie-backed models, initialize it with runtime configuration, add instructions and prompts, optionally register local tools discovered through Java ServiceLoader, execute requests, and collect usage metrics for monitoring. Core capabilities include a common Genai contract for prompt-based interactions, an EmbeddingProvider contract for vector generation used in semantic search and similarity workflows, GenaiProviderManager for dynamic provider and embedding provider resolution, concrete integrations for OpenAI-compatible APIs, Anthropic Claude, and EPAM CodeMie, and a ToolsProvider for structured host-defined tool invocation. The OpenAI integration supports Responses API requests, tool/function calling, optional built-in web search, MCP server integration, and embedding generation. The Anthropic integration supports message-based prompt execution, tool loops, optional web search and MCP forwarding, and usage tracking. The CodeMie integration authenticates with OAuth 2.0, configures downstream OpenAI-compatible or Anthropic-compatible providers, and exposes embeddings through delegated providers. The tools subsystem defines annotations, descriptors, execution contracts, and loading utilities for exposing Java methods and prompts as AI-accessible tools. This artifact is suited for AI-enabled Java applications that need vendor-neutral LLM access, embeddings for semantic search or content assembly, runtime tool registration, and centralized usage accounting within the Machanism ecosystem.

usageContext: Java applications that need a provider-neutral generative AI client API
Machai and Machanism ecosystem modules requiring prompt execution and token accounting
Applications implementing semantic search or similarity features using embeddings
Systems that expose curated host-side functions to AI models through tool calling
Enterprise integrations that must switch between OpenAI, Anthropic, and CodeMie-compatible backends with minimal application code changes

constructors.description: Primary entry point for creating a request-scoped generative AI provider. The method resolves provider/model identifiers such as OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini, Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet, or a fully qualified provider class name, instantiates the corresponding provider implementation, and initializes it with runtime configuration including credentials, endpoints, and execution limits.

examples.description: Resolves and initializes an OpenAI-backed provider using the configured runtime properties. After creation, the returned provider can receive instructions, prompts, and optional tools before request execution.

constructors.description: Factory method for creating an embedding provider using the same provider resolution strategy as chat providers. Use this when the application needs vector embeddings for semantic search, ranking, clustering, or similarity comparison.

examples.description: Creates an embedding provider for OpenAI-compatible embedding generation so application code can compute vectors without binding to a vendor SDK.

constructors.description: Creates a loader that discovers FunctionTools implementations from the classpath through java.util.ServiceLoader. The loader is then used to register curated host-side functions and prompt definitions with a compatible Genai provider.

examples.description: Instantiates the tool loader before applying discovered tools to a request-scoped provider.

features.name: getProvider
features.description: Dynamically resolves and initializes a concrete Genai implementation from a provider:model identifier or fully qualified class name. This is the core feature for vendor-neutral provider selection and lets application code switch among supported backends without changing its interaction flow.

examples.description: Shows an end-to-end provider resolution and prompt execution flow using the provider-neutral API. The same pattern can be used with different provider:model identifiers to switch backends.

features.name: getEmbeddingProvider
features.description: Creates and initializes an EmbeddingProvider for generating dense vector embeddings from text. This feature is intended for semantic search, similarity lookup, content clustering, and other retrieval-augmented scenarios.

examples.description: Generates an embedding vector that can be stored in a vector index for semantic search over project or document content.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes the currently accumulated request against the selected provider and returns the final text response. Provider implementations may run multi-step tool-call loops, process attached inputs, and capture token usage before returning the result.

examples.description: Runs a request using the provider-neutral contract after instructions and prompt text have been added.

features.name: embedding
features.description: Computes an embedding vector for input text through a provider-specific backend while keeping the calling code independent of the underlying SDK. This is a foundational capability for semantic retrieval and content similarity operations.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getEmbeddingProvider("OpenAI:text-embedding-3-large", null);
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> embedding = provider.embedding("Searchable representation of a package description", 256L);
examples.description: Computes a vector representation of text so that semantically similar content can later be retrieved by nearest-neighbor search.

features.name: applyTools
features.description: Discovers compatible FunctionTools implementations and registers their tools and prompts with a Genai provider. This feature enables controlled host-side capabilities such as file access, command execution, HTTP retrieval, or domain-specific actions to be exposed to supporting AI providers.

examples.description: Registers discovered host-side tools before execution so the model can call local capabilities during the response loop.

features.name: addTool
features.signature: public void org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider.addTool(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String description, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction function, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ParamDescriptor... paramsDesc)
features.description: Registers a function tool with the OpenAI-backed provider. The provider converts the declared parameters into a JSON Schema object so the model can decide when and how to call the tool during a response loop.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider provider = new org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider();
provider.addTool("fetchStatus", "Returns external status information", null, new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ParamDescriptor());
examples.description: Illustrates where OpenAI-specific tool registration happens. In practice, applications more commonly rely on FunctionToolsLoader to discover and apply tool definitions automatically.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
customizations.description: Primary extension point for contributing host-side function tools and prompt definitions. Implementations are discovered through Java ServiceLoader and can install domain-specific capabilities that are selectively exposed to AI providers.

examples.description: Represents a custom FunctionTools implementation that can be packaged and discovered from the classpath to provide application-specific tools.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
customizations.description: Application-facing abstraction that can be implemented or adapted to support additional generative AI backends. Developers can extend the library by providing new Genai implementations that honor the common lifecycle for initialization, prompt collection, execution, and cleanup.

examples.description: Shows the shape of a custom provider implementation that can integrate another model vendor while remaining compatible with the GenAI Client programming model.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
customizations.description: Extension point for alternative embedding backends. Implementations can provide vector generation for provider-specific models while remaining consumable through the same application-facing API.

examples.description: Illustrates how developers can plug in an alternative embedding backend for specialized models or internal AI platforms.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
studs.description: Core provider contract for generative AI integrations. Implementations manage request initialization, prompt accumulation, instruction handling, tool registration, execution, logging, and lifecycle reset.

examples.description: Obtains a concrete implementation of the Genai contract through the manager so client code can remain provider-agnostic.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
studs.description: Contract for embedding backends that initialize from application configuration and generate numeric vector representations for input text.

examples.description: Retrieves an implementation of the embedding contract for semantic search or similarity scenarios.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
studs.description: SPI marker interface for tool installer classes that contribute callable host-side capabilities to AI providers via ServiceLoader discovery.

examples.description: Defines a tool contributor that can be discovered and applied to a provider at runtime.

examples.title: Basic provider-neutral chat request
examples.scenario: Use the library to send instructions and a prompt to a concrete provider resolved at runtime.
steps: Create or obtain an org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator containing provider credentials, model, endpoint, and timeout settings.
Resolve the provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider using a value such as OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini or Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet.
Set system behavior with instructions and add one or more user prompts.
Call perform() to execute the request and read the final text response.
Optionally clear the provider instance after use because providers are request-scoped and mutable.
examples.description: This example demonstrates the primary application flow: resolve a provider, accumulate request content, execute the prompt, and then reset request state.

examples.title: Generate embeddings for semantic search
examples.scenario: Create embeddings for text fragments that will later be indexed for semantic retrieval.
steps: Prepare a Configurator with credentials for an embedding-capable backend such as OpenAI or CodeMie.
Pass the text to embedding() together with the desired vector dimensionality supported by the backend.
Store the returned vector in a vector index or similarity-search structure.
examples.description: Use this workflow when implementing semantic search, document clustering, similarity ranking, or retrieval-augmented generation pipelines.

examples.title: Expose host-side tools to an AI provider
examples.scenario: Install and use locally implemented tools discovered from the classpath so the model can invoke controlled host capabilities.
steps: Implement one or more org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools contributors and package them so Java ServiceLoader can discover them.
Resolve a Genai provider that supports tool execution.
Create org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader and apply tools to the provider with the application class as a compatibility filter.
Send a prompt that may trigger one of the registered tools and execute the request with perform().
examples.description: This scenario is appropriate when a generative AI workflow must interact with local files, HTTP endpoints, shell commands, or application-specific services through controlled tool calls.

examples.title: Use CodeMie as a delegated enterprise provider
examples.scenario: Authenticate against EPAM CodeMie and delegate requests to a downstream OpenAI-compatible or Anthropic-compatible backend.
steps: Configure GENAI_USERNAME and GENAI_PASSWORD in the application's configuration source. Use an e-mail style username for password grant or a client id for client-credentials grant.
Optionally configure AUTH_URL when the default token endpoint must be overridden.
Resolve the provider using a CodeMie-prefixed model string or initialize org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.codemie.CodeMieProvider directly.
Send prompts through the provider-neutral Genai API and let the CodeMie provider fetch an access token and configure the delegated backend.
examples.description: Use this approach in enterprise environments where CodeMie provides centralized authentication and managed access to downstream AI models.
